---
title: "Verify A Claim"
---

Use this page when you want to check whether a sentence, diagram, widget, paper paragraph, or theorem card is backed by the formal project.

## Quick Rule

A mathematical claim is Lean-proved only when all of these are true:

1. The claim has a theorem id in the [theorem index](theorems.qmd).
2. The theorem status badge says `Lean-proved`.
3. The theorem row links to a Lean declaration source.
4. The theorem id appears in the generated paper and dictionary backlinks expected for that claim.
5. The repository checks pass locally or in GitHub Actions.

If any of those are missing, read the claim as planned, exploratory, draft, blocked, deferred, or explanatory.

::: {.warning-box}
Widgets, diagrams, Python examples, target entries, and paper prose are not proofs. They are useful only when they stay downstream of theorem manifests and compiled Lean declarations.
:::

## Trace A Theorem Id

Given a theorem id such as `CC-T0005`:

1. Open [Theorem Index](theorems.qmd#CC-T0005).
2. Check the status badge.
3. Open the Lean source link if the theorem is Lean-proved.
4. Follow the paper backlinks to see where the theorem is used in prose.
5. Follow the dictionary links to check the meanings of the terms used by the theorem.

The generated theorem index is a navigation layer. The formal proof source remains the Lean declaration compiled by `lake build`.

## Trace A Dictionary Term

Given a dictionary id such as `CC-0001`:

1. Open [Dictionary Index](dictionary.qmd#CC-0001).
2. Read the formal definition and forbidden meanings.
3. Follow theorem, paper, widget, and glyph backlinks.
4. Check whether the term is used in a proved theorem, a draft paper, an exploratory target, or only a widget.

Dictionary entries control vocabulary. They do not prove theorem statements by themselves.

## Trace A Paper Claim

Given a paper id or title:

1. Open [Paper Index](papers.qmd).
2. Filter by the paper id or title.
3. Open the paper source and sidecar links.
4. Follow every theorem id listed for the paper into the theorem index.
5. Treat prose without a proved theorem id as exposition, motivation, roadmap, or conjectural framing.

## Trace An Advertised Capability

Use the [Proof-Backed Showcase](showcase.qmd) when the claim is not a single theorem but a portfolio lane such as `SHOW-001` or `SHOW-010`.

The checklist below is generated from `site/data/generated/capability_showcase.json`, which is exported from `manifests/capability_showcase.yaml`. A capability is ready to advertise only when its generated claim contract passes: role, standard anchor, Circle expression, Circle-native value, advertised claim, proof scope, proof provenance, paper ids, theorem ids, dictionary ids, source refs, executable refs, reproduction commands, Living Book refs, advertising-language guardrails, and not-claimed boundary must all be present and in sync with that manifest. The generated audit also exposes route, portfolio, per-theorem, per-source, and Living Book presentation backing contracts: each advertised theorem id must be proved and carried by a cited paper, each advertised source ref must be backed by a cited paper Source Trail or cited Lean sidecar import closure, each advertised Living Book page must carry the capability reference plus declared widgets or theorem cards, each portfolio route must aggregate only ready known capabilities, positive advertising text must avoid unsupported overclaim patterns while boundary text must be explicit, and the portfolio-level backing summary must match the generated capability rows.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="capability_audit_checklist"></div>

## Trace A Widget Or Glyph

For a widget:

1. Check the widget page warning.
2. Check the theorem id attached to the widget panel, if any.
3. Open the theorem id in the theorem index.
4. Compare the widget output with the Python reference only as executable support.

For a glyph:

1. Open [Proof-Carrying Glyphs](chapters/phase2/proof_carrying_glyphs.qmd).
2. Filter or inspect the glyph id.
3. Follow the linked theorem id and dictionary ids.
4. Check the glyph status against the theorem status.

Glyphs are proof interfaces. They do not replace Lean proof checking.

## Local Verification Commands

Use the full local gate when changing proofs, manifests, dictionary entries, papers, widgets, or site data:

```bash
make living-book-check
```

This includes Lean build, repository checks, site data export, Living Book source checks, Quarto render, and rendered artifact validation.

For focused Living Book work:

```bash
make sitecheck
make site-render
make site-render-check
```

`make sitecheck` includes theorem id checks, dictionary id checks, paper links, source links, reciprocal generated backlinks, theorem-status language checks, widget mount contracts, widget accessibility contracts, and widget/Python parity.

## Public Verification

The public site is hosted at [Circle Calculus Living Book](https://corbensorenson.github.io/circle-calculus/). It is published from GitHub Pages only after the deployment workflow builds Lean, runs source checks, renders Quarto, validates the rendered artifact, and uploads `site/_site/`.

The public page is a learning resource. The proof source is still the repository state that produced it.

<script type="module" src="widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
<script type="module" src="widgets/showcase/capability_audit_checklist.js"></script>
